dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Unnamed_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, whaddaya know, we got ourselves a visitor, ladies! Who woulda thought? You found us, sweetie! You get, I don't know, three questions? Three of us, three questions, sounds reasonable, right?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You're... you're the Fates, you're... really here! Are you safe, in any distress? Your brother Lord Moros, he's searched everywhere for you, and not just him!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: Are you safe, in any distress? Whew! What do you think, ladies, we count that as one question or two? I say go easy on her, seems like a nice girl. Count it just as one, OK?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_02
dialogueLines.text: Oh yeah, nice girl, you're right, count it as one! OK!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: OK! We're fine, sweetie, least now we are! Your grandpa got us, yeah, we were a little miffed, you know? 'Cause we'd been weaving, and weaving, and weaving, so then we figured: What's a better time to take a break?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Take a break... shouldn't you have sent word to Lord Moros, much less anybody else? He's been very concerned!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: That's question two! We did send word, that's why you're here! Moros, he's a real sweetheart. We figured he could use a little alone time... OK, last question!

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Last question, I... what do you all intend to do, now that Chronos no longer seems to be a threat? How do we prevent something like this from happening again?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: OK now that I'm counting as two questions so I'm only gonna give you the first one, or maybe it was the second, I'm getting mixed up, point is: We're not gonna do a thing...! This has been nice.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Not going to do a thing, and I'm unable to ask further questions, so... perhaps you're going to elaborate...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: Hey, who knows what's gonna happen, you or me? Look, all you gods, you got yourselves into this. I know, I know, you think it's 'cause of us, well, we don't like that kind of pressure! And you've been doing OK on your own, so...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_02
dialogueLines.text: Yeah you've been doing OK, haha! So we are gonna let ya keep this up!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: We're gonna let you keep this up. And once you've had your fill, being without us for a while, you're gonna hand it over to mortals. Just like we're handing it over to you!

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Hand what over to mortals... which is to say... I don't know how I can respond to that without another question.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: That's all right, sweetie! Think of it like, uh... you gods can do your own thing for a while, then mortals can do theirs! It'll be a brand new age! Been a while since the last one of those.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: So you're retiring... all three of you. No more weavings... we're to keep fending for ourselves, come what may.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh come on, don't make it sound like such a big deal! Not like you're gonna do anything different anyway. But you could! Just ask your old teacher sometime. Now she's pulled some strings, let me tell you!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_02
dialogueLines.text: Oh yeah Teach has definitely pulled some strings all right, huhuh!

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You mean Headmistress. So you're saying... I don't know what you're saying, but I'll ask. As you well know, I couldn't have done any of this without her.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_03
dialogueLines.text: Guuuhhhhh, ngghhh...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Fates_01
dialogueLines.text: Ohh, yeah, we know. Well anyway, sweetie, we're gonna cut ya loose, as we've got other things to do! But not weaving! Not for the foreseeable future, as they say, heh. So nice to finally meet ya. Bye!

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Erm, bye...! And, thank you all!

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros, I've a friend who lives in the woods called Arachne. Long story, but she was on the receiving end of a powerful curse that... changed her into a spider. Do you deal in curses at all?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I remember the case of Arachne. I didn't know what became of her after her metamorphosis, so... it's interesting to learn that you are friends. But no... I'm afraid curses aren't my specialty.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You called it the case of Arachne. She was thought to be the greatest weaver in the world, greater than the gods... greater even than the Fates? Unwelcome competition, perhaps...?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I've come to value our companionship, Princess, so there's something you should know about me in case it becomes pertinent to us... I'm promised to another, by the Fates.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Promised to another, as in, you are betrothed? Well then, congratulations are in order I should think! Though to whom, if I may ask...? If the Fates even let on.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, at the risk of being overly direct, I've concluded that she isn't you. The Fates described her as the morning light, whose luminance breaks through the darkness and reveals the beauty of the world. You're... well, different than that.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh... that could be Eos, I suppose. I know her sister Selene the Moon rather well, but am not very familiar with Dawn. Are you supposed to seek her out?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I fear I do not know. She and I are to meet at some point, but apparently not yet. When first you and I met, I thought perhaps... that I might find her in this place.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: It's dark here; I don't think you shall.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: About this goddess to whom you're betrothed. If she really is Eos the incarnate of Dawn, perhaps I could facilitate an introduction through her sister, if you like?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh, um, well, I wouldn't want for you to go out of your way, or for Selene, for that matter. You have a vital task, and I know that the Moon herself is aiding you.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: But, you said you thought you might find her here. Perhaps the Three Fates knew you'd come to us eventually, and that I'd set you on the path to meet your love?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, if this prophecy concerning me and the one to whom I have been promised hasn't appeared on the Fated List, then there truly is no need for you to intervene.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You don't sound all that enthusiastic about it. Do you not want to meet this goddess at some point?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: We are bound to meet; whether I want to or not is irrelevant.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You took some interest in this goddess to whom I am said to be bound, presuming she is Eos, the incarnate of Dawn. The truth is, when the Fates first told me about her, I grew rather frustrated.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I would have probably been frustrated as well. Did your sisters foresee this union, or arrange it? Perhaps they sought to unite your powerful family with that of a Titaness.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: In typical fashion, they revealed little. But the way in which they kept describing this goddess, or Titaness, and telling me how contented I would be... it was the first time I questioned their wisdom. Not outwardly, of course.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: They probably meant well, but... what is it you intend to do?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If I am doomed to be with Dawn, then so be it. But I shall not be forcing that outcome along. I would much prefer to remain here... if you'll have me.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: The choice ought to be yours.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You sing beautifully, Princess. One of the Three Fates likes to hum while she weaves, but sounds nothing like you.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It's mostly Artemis whose voice carries so strongly through this clearing, though thank you. We sometimes sing like this in preparation for the night.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The goddess of the hunt and you seem rather close. The other Olympians less so, true?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Besides Lord Hermes, I met most of the others one by one around the time you first showed up.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: A Pitch-Black Stone has been here this entire time, and I failed to detect it. I fear I'm beginning to slip up more often than acceptable.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It was inert just recently, Lord Moros. Until your grandparent gave me an idea for how to see the possibilities within. Though, perhaps I ought have checked with you first. You're troubled...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, it's just... Chaos and my sisters didn't always get along. Chaos wanted everything to be possible... while the Fates, their desire is for things a certain way. But, they would all agree that you should use the Stone as you see fit.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Nothing like a Pitch-Black Stone to bring the family together, right?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How well would you say you know your grandparent, Lord Moros? I'd not expected to meet Primordial Chaos of all beings. But they summoned me to their domain, and... I don't quite know what they want from me.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Chaos summoned you...? That is unusual, though not entirely without precedent. And in this case, I can imagine why. Chaos and Mother Nyx were rather close, after all.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: So Chaos seeks their daughter. They did mention something about a quandary.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: This isn't the first time they took an interest in your family. Or took action that the Fates themselves likely never preordained. I choose to think you've made a new ally.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I learned what happened to the Fates, Lord Moros. Or rather, how Chronos discovered where they were. It was Chaos who led Chronos to them. Chaos, of course, seems to regret this now, and I don't think had any ill intent...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh. That is a lot of information to take in. It does make some amount of sense. Chronos, having free reign in your father's House, eventually found his way to Chaos, then coaxed this vital information out of them. Then, he ambushed the Fates, as part of his plans for conquest.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You're taking all this better than I would have. Come to think of it, I've never seen you angry.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You're seeing me right now. It's just, my anger changes nothing, typically. So Chaos now is working to undo some of the damage caused? Chronos removed one threat in the Fates, but created another. Perhaps an unwise trade.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Making a mockery of Chaos seems unwise to me!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: How far were you able to go last night, Princess? You were gone for some time. I sensed the growing fear and excitement in the air, even heard some of the rumblings.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: If the rumblings were that I reached the depths of Tartarus, broke into my father's stolen House, confronted Chronos there, and outfought him, then you heard correctly. Hardly a final victory, but still.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I would object to anyone but you diminishing such a victory. Is this not a vitally important component of the task you've been entrusted to complete?

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Then congratulations, and may the Fates avail your next move.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Chronos fell to you once more, that much I heard. It's an impressive feat, although... you mean to bring him death, yet he is deathless. This seems to be a paradox.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: That's right. Mortal death is not the end, but mortals often think it is. When we say Death to Chronos, we mean it in that absolute way. As impossible as it may seem.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: So then, to vanquish him must be at least a step toward that end. What shall become of everything if you succeed and Time is truly killed? A world without Time...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...A world without ruin and decay; our world existed before Time and can exist without.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Another paradox, yet one that must be true.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Hail, Lord Moros. How have you been acclimating so far? Is there something we can do to make your stay more comfortable?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: No, Princess, I'm well, and acclimating, as you say. I've never been to such a place as this! And making every effort not to be a nuisance here.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If you don't mind me asking, Princess: Have you ever died before? Not in the mortal sense, it's just... you seem to have exceptional tenacity in moments most gods couldn't endure.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I cannot afford to lose my grip on consciousness out there. Headmistress taught me always to return to shadows with my final bit of strength. I learned the hard way, but I learned.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It shows. Remarkable, to have met someone equally as comfortable with death as well as life. I practically see it in your eyes.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What did you use to do, Lord Moros? I mean before all this. I have only the vaguest sense of it.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I served my sisters the Three Fates, both as their advocate, and as their guardian. The Fates determine when mortal lives draw to a close, and also sometimes how. I would bear witness to these more-specific weavings...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The mortals must have appreciated your diligence. Has somebody else since taken over your responsibilities?

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Heh! You could say that, I suppose.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The Shades around here... I know they've been avoiding you, Lord Moros. Some are filled with fear. Others with contempt. You ended their lives?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It never was for me to decide why a mortal's life should suddenly be cut short. But, I did get to choose the how of it. Knowing the future could be monotonous work, so my sisters and I tried to keep things... interesting.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You brought misery to mortals because you were bored?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm not proud of it. Especially not when you put it that way. And I regret I've caused dissension in your ranks.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You said something before to me, Princess. Concerning mortalkind. That surely they appreciate my diligence, because I'm with them in the final moments of their lives. The truth of it, however, is they hate me; most of them. In life, as well as death.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I appreciate your candor. What you say aligns with some of the rumblings I've heard from Shades loyal to us. Though it's strange, Lord Moros. Your reputation is one thing; but you, it seems, are entirely another.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Heh! From you, Princess, I'll take that as a compliment.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What were some of your favorite examples of the work you and your sisters did? Particularly inventive ends delivered with theatrics, anything like that?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh, where to start? Children slaughtering their parents was an old standby of ours; I'm reluctant to choose our best. There also was the war from which I first took notice of Master Odysseus. That had some good ones.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The part where mortals perished in the throes of agony and terror, leaving behind loved ones to bitterly mourn their loss... you were unmoved by all of that?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: We have a responsibility to the realm that takes precedence. And besides, mortals don't realize that everything ends up all right for most of them.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps we haven't done enough to promote the way the Underworld operates. Or used to.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: As long as mortals fear us still.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Your companion Dora... she's a lively one. Especially for her age. Most of the dead grow quite lethargic over time. Although I suppose things have been more eventful recently.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Dora seems to be quite intrigued by you, Lord Moros. Call it a morbid fascination. You mention her age... she doesn't seem to have any recollection of such details. But, you can just tell?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I have my ways. Dora is... well, she's not the eldest one here, though she's been around longer than most. That much is safe for me to divulge.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Something seems to have occurred with your companion Dora. She hasn't been as keen to make her remarks or possess my belongings. Is she all right?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Yes, I think so. Something's been nagging at her for a while that she's finally started to sort out. She may well tell you more about it at some point.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I think I may already know. Well, she's an old soul but a strong one. And fortunate to have you as a friend. Do you suppose there's something I may do to help?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Act frightened from her hauntings. She loves that.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: While you were out, the Fated List... it started shaking, and scrolled over to a prophecy I'd never seen, and already fulfilled. I think there may be more to it, but...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You're not supposed to tell me any more than that, I know. I think I have an idea of what that prophecy concerned, as the Fated List revealed some more to me as well.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm attempting to understand what could have prompted such a change. You'll follow where the List is leading, yes?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'd ask what my sister Eris is doing here, but then they say wheresoever goes Doom, Strife follows. Or the other way around, whether I like it or not. Has she been here long?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: She comes and goes from time to time, typically after some bloody battle between the forces of Chronos and Olympus. We first met a while back. Briefly trained together though Headmistress soon gave up on her, as patient as she is.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The Fates gave up on her as well. Ordered that we never interact, that I not clean her messes anymore; although sometimes she'll goad a conversation out of me. I trust she hasn't made your lives too difficult?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Eris outright attacked me, just as I was approaching Mount Olympus. Who gave her the Adamant Rail, anyway? A weapon once used to slay Titans, turned against the gods...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The Rail has turned against the gods before. Only the Fates know its true and final purpose... but Eris has it by their own decree. Perhaps as a plaything to keep her occupied.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: To keep me occupied, apparently. Makes me wonder if the Fates want Chronos stopped, or not. Did you and Eris ever fight like this?

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Oh, yes. Much as I tried to avoid it.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros... I understand you can't reveal the weavings of the Fates. But, could you just tell me what the three of them are like?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: My sisters. They... well each is her own goddess, I suppose. Clotho the spinstress is the talkative one, the decisive one. Lachesis is a little more agreeable; she apportions the thread. And Atropos, she sticks to the cutting.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It must be a little imposing, knowing your sisters determine the course of every life. Even your own?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...My task began immediately following reports that Chronos found a way to gain the upper hand against Olympus. We just didn't know which sort of way. But, now I think I do: your sisters the Three Fates... he got them, didn't he? Is that why you're here?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...You ask while making clear to me that you already know. I ought to have been there when it happened. Perhaps the Fates knew I lacked the strength to protect them; so they protected me.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How did he find them? What does he want from them?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I don't know how he found them. Few beings with knowledge of their whereabouts exist. As to what he wants from them... and this is a conjecture of my own... surely he'll try to influence their weavings from here on.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros, you said before you think Chronos is going to influence the weavings of the Fates. You mean he's going to try to change the future? Force a favorable outcome?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Yes. I think he's already trying to do just that. Unfortunately for him, the Three Fates can be a bit headstrong. I trust they may be quite resistant as captives.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I can't imagine Chronos tolerating insolence, whether from your sisters or anybody else. How he must hate them! When he was overthrown before, that was the outcome of a fated prophecy...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Indeed. As for now, I sense there are no weavings happening at all. No new futures foretold. Chronos believes he has no prophecy to fear. Yet, I believe this is an opportunity for you. To make a better future for us all.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Your family has been of keen interest to the Fates for some time, hasn't it? Ever since Chronos learned he was doomed to be usurped by his own children.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: A prophecy that was only fulfilled because Chronos learned of it, and desperately tried to prevent it from coming to pass. Perhaps I'd have an easier time with the Fated List if I try to thwart it at every turn.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I wouldn't second-guess too much. Later your father and uncles drew lots to divide up their rule of the world. By chance, your father wound up with the least desirable part, didn't he?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps the Underworld best suited him. So now it's my turn to see that the will of the Fates is done? If they even planned this far ahead.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: They were captured, so this can't all be according to their plan. But they are counting on your aid.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How can you be so certain that there are no more weavings taking place? That rather than the Fates setting the course of our lives, there's no pattern anymore... only sheer, mindless chance?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I never said I was certain... only that I no longer sensed my sisters' work, and it's a relatively well-honed sense. But, I've also never been apart from my sisters like this. I feel... unmoored.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: As though you don't know where you're going. To me, everything feels the same as ever. Present circumstances notwithstanding. How much control did the Fates truly have?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: They are highly influential, I can tell you that. But can they force your every action, every thought? Of course not. You are conscious. It was your choice to speak with me just now.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: But the Fates knew we'd meet.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You used to know everything that was going to occur, didn't you, Lord Moros? Up until recently, from what I gathered. I can't imagine...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, imagine it being rather dull, always knowing what's to come. But now I'm no different than you. How do you... get by like this? Many surprises are unwelcome, yet there also can be pleasant ones, true?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm sure it helps that I don't know it any other way. This must be very difficult for you.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Something the matter, Lord Moros? You seem more ill-at-ease than usual. If there's additional bad news you're at liberty to share, I'm listening.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh, haha! It's nothing of the sort, at least for now, Princess. It's just... this sensation of uncertainty. Not just in the grander scheme, but even from one evening to the next... I'm still unused to it.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: There can be pleasure in the unexpected. But even those of us who never had the gift of foresight struggle in the way that you describe. For me, it helps to focus on a present state of mind. In being here right now.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I've been attempting what you said before, to focus on a present state of mind. It's proving difficult, because... my mind invariably drifts to many possibilities of negative outcomes.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm familiar with that feeling. We have to consider favorable outcomes side-by-side, and plan for them. Then, at a certain point, all we can do is hope...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Hope... I much prefer a sense of certainty. It's odd to think that my sisters the Fates, to others, represent sheer chance. Matters beyond one's control. Yet to me, they made everything so clear... I'd rather trust than hope.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: All these thoughtful furnishings nearby... thank you, Princess. Your companion Dora asked me a variety of questions about my preferences, but I didn't realize you would accommodate me in this way.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: As you've been staying with us for a little while now, you may as well have a degree of comfort here. Though surely it's nothing compared to what you had serving the Fates.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh, where I used to reside was more configured to my sisters' tastes. Although considering how much I've come to like spindles and drapery, perhaps mine aren't far off.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I've not seen the Witch of the Crossroads recently, Princess. Do you know where she may be?

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: I have an idea, yes. I have to go.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The phrase you told me ended up revealing a new Aspect of the Umbral Flames, a dark god known as Supay. Is that name at all familiar to you?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Not until now. The Fates presumably chose each and every bearer of the Arms of Night. Those bearers may not be bound by blood, but are connected still. And it seems I was to be your link to this Supay...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How can it be that there are Underworld gods that neither of us know? Most of them must be your mother's side.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I couldn't say. Perhaps there shall be other Underworlds besides our own. Or there already are.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I noticed Hypnos was no longer in his spot, though for how long, I couldn't say. Perhaps Odysseus had him relocated someplace where the snoring would be less of a distraction.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh no, I woke him up. Required a series of increasingly complex alchemical processes and dreamwalks, but still. He immediately left for the House of Hades in a bit of a panic.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm sorry I missed him! From what I know about your father, he does value punctuality. And from what I know about my brother Sleep, he must have greeted you enthusiastically.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: That winged Shade you speak to on some nights... I recall his case, and am surprised a simple boy who died too young found his way here. He is an asset to your cause?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Icarus is no mere asset. He's the apprentice of great Daedalus, as well as someone dear to me. I think he may remember you as well.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: A mortal's death often results in them forgetting me, though not always. I'm pleased that Icarus did not allow the circumstances of his passing to dictate the rest of his existence. Do you recommend I greet him?

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Probably best to leave him be.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: That tends to be the case.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The artifact I gave to you... it can forestall the inevitable. Almost like a Doom Repellant... except, it works in the opposite way. Such technicalities aside, I trust it shall be of some benefit.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm certain it shall. Sometimes all I need is a few moments more. So if I use your Pin, you get to accompany me on my evenings out?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Only in the final moments of your evenings, I'm afraid. Of course, we also have the evenings here...

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Then I'll be seeing you around.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I figured that you knew something of the Nocturnal Arms, Lord Moros. But your connection to Ygnium, I'd not have guessed. You once wielded the Umbral Flames yourself! I revealed an Aspect of them that could only be your own.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, I take it you're not saying this for sake of jest, so please believe in turn that I have never used the Flames before; I know them only by reputation. Can you be certain that this Aspect is my own? I've many relatives...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I... yes, I think it is! The Nocturnal Arms bond with their bearers in such a way that their connections to other bearers become almost like shared memories. Although...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...Although, these glimpses can be both of bearers past, and still to come, true? So it may be that I shall bear those Flames in some other age in which the realm's in need. I... trust the Fates must have wanted me to know in advance.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: So when did you want to start learning the Umbral Flames, Lord Moros? Perhaps a lesson is in order at some point.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: As tempting as that sounds, I don't think I ought to be borrowing the Arms of Night. I don't want to make too much a habit of defying my sisters, even if they're not around.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What do you suppose will be the reason I eventually hand over the Flames to you, then? Or it may not even be me...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: A situation similar to yours; those I care about in need, the Flames available. I'd take them up unhesitatingly.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Forgive me if I overstep, Princess. It's just... it seems to me that you and Nemesis don't always get along...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Whatever could have given you such an impression, Lord Moros? Perhaps all our incessant squabbles over petty things?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: They can't be petty things if they're of your concern, or for that matter, hers. I understand there's some dispute about your task. You were chosen for it, she was not. True?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: That's true. Nemesis trained hard, but so have I. The choice was never mine, yet she blames me.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If I know Nemesis, she blames herself. Although, she takes it out on you, that I can see. If anyone, she ought to blame the Fates...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You and Nemesis appear to have been interacting in a more productive way of late. If I may ask, what changed between you two?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I think we're becoming more comfortable with our respective roles in all of this. And perhaps Nemesis has finally gained a modicum of faith in my ability.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I see... although I think there's something more. Her respect for you doesn't seem newly formed.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You must know the Three Fates better than anyone, besides maybe Nyx, herself! Erm, though, forgive me for invoking her...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It's quite all right. The Fates, I know full well, but Mother Nyx...? We saw her seldomly. The nature of our work was such that even she would almost never get involved. Besides, she'd many other children to attend to.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm sorry to hear that. My mother's the Queen. We've never even met since I was an infant. Nobody around here seems to know their blasted mothers, come to think!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Come now, the mothers can't be blamed. We children are insufferable at times.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm attempting to contain my enthusiasm for the venerable hero of mortalkind over there, Princess. Our adversary may be crafty, but Odysseus is renowned for his wiles above all!

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It figures you would be familiar with the Great Tactician! Likely he evaded you at least once or twice in his life, if his many tales are to be believed.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Oh, they're to be believed, I can attest.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Another victory within the depths of Tartarus... except now, your fight requires you to transcend space and time, with assistance from your grandfather, of course. True...?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: True, as far as I can understand, Lord Moros. Headmistress notified you of these new details, then? You don't sound terribly surprised.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I assure you that my inability to sound terribly surprised does not prevent my feeling so. You have many such battles left to fight?

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Then to your many victories to come.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Are your attempts to scale the mountain of the gods less difficult at least, now than before? The war with Chronos having ended surely must bring some relief.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Well on that front, not too much has changed as yet. Apparently it's important for the stalemate between the Titan's legions and Olympus to persist, for sake of our clean-up efforts.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: So then your fight continues as before, except without a burning vengeance for your family to compel you. What is the motivating factor now?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh, where to start... I have some matters that I wish to see resolved. And, just to see what happens, I suppose!

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: I, too, increasingly have the desire to see that.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You must know the Titan Prometheus. I faced him on Olympus. Little wonder he now fights against the gods, having met his doom and lived... unlike the mortals he supposedly cares for so much.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Prometheus... I took no pleasure in his plight. The gods certainly know how to make an example of someone. Now he seeks vengeance? A favored pattern in the weavings of the Fates.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Now that you mention it, his knowledge of the future seems similar to that of your sisters. Do you suppose he's sided with Chronos because he knows Chronos will ultimately win?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I do not wish to speculate, Princess. All I know is the Titan of Foresight never was the violent sort. His experience in captivity... must have changed him.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: I'll have to change him some more.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: In my weakest moments... I used to envy Prometheus, even knowing his plight. For he was loved by mortals, having given them the gift of fire from Olympus. I, on the other hand, was hated. It should not have mattered to me... but it did.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Come on, Lord Moros. What mortals think of us is of no consequence. Even if it were, better to be hated than a traitor. Prometheus thinks only of mortals and himself. I'm surprised Chronos would entrust anything to him.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Although I said I didn't wish to speculate, I... cannot shake the sense that Chronos values him because so many mortals do. The new Golden Age he seeks to bring about... an age for mortals, not for gods...

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Doubtless all the mortals' problems would be solved...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If I may, Princess? The Fated List of Minor Prophecies... have you been able to make use of it?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I have. Thank you for bringing it to me. So then, the Fates already know what's going to happen in all this? Everything I'm going to do?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, yes and no. The Fated List says as little of your future as necessary. I briefly was its host myself, and saw there but a single prophecy... that I would pass it on to you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The Fated List continues to unfold, so thank you. While the Fates often let events unfold naturally, at times they value an additional degree of certainty. And are willing to make it worthwhile.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I've seen a number of the listed prophecies already come to pass. Still others are foretold in their place. The magick in that scroll, it's beyond anything I've learned...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You must have your doubts; the Fates are rather famous for their sense of irony. But, no matter the outcome, I'm grateful for your willingness to discover their design. Our families are at stake.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I confess I feel a bit self-conscious that the Fated List is there for you and all to see. Some of the matters it describes are rather personal.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If it's any comfort, the prophecies appear only to you. We see there only what's meant for us. Although, when you fulfill a prophecy... I can usually tell. It's like a sudden realization.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: We know our paths are intertwined to an extent... well, may your sudden realizations connected to my actions not be too embarrassing.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You know, some of these Fated prophecies are rather substantial. I shudder to think what might be on a list of major prophecies...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I trust this doesn't make you feel as though your service hasn't been of value, because certainly it has. However, the prophecies listed there are indeed minor, in the grand scheme of things.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The grand scheme of things... as in all existence, the unfathomable past, unknowable future... that sort of grandeur, you mean?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Yes. The Fates preside over the smallest minutiae and the greatest of patterns. So don't take the idea of minor prophecies the wrong way. A small ripple can become a great wave.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Your father once was bearer of the Fated List, you know. A House-warming gift, my sisters said. But Hades put it away... until your brother unearthed it from the archives, and started using it himself.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I know Zagreus sought our mother... perhaps the Fated List helped him succeed. Come to think, I wouldn't have even been born if he hadn't. Wait... how did you end up with the List after that?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It wasn't until rather recently. The Fates sent me to retrieve the List from your father's House. Chronos would be absent, they said. And they were right; for when I returned, they were gone.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Some nights I'm on the surface, I remember I'm defying the will of the Fates simply by being there. Yet the Fated List has certain prophecies that cannot be fulfilled anywhere else. Your sisters, they... must have anticipated this.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It certainly is possible, even probable. Yet, historically, my sisters have had little patience for those who disregard their prophecies. Perhaps they're making an exception.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Or perhaps I'm inviting some terrible consequence. Although if Chronos didn't disobey their prophecies, I wouldn't have either.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: In my experience it's best to avoid dwelling on such subject matter. Not even I can always see the pattern in my sisters' reasoning, and I think that's their preference.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You... haven't often looked upon the Fated List of late. For many nights, I would anticipate your safe return, then watch as you fulfilled one prophecy after another. Now you brush right by it to come speak with me.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros, at the risk of profoundly disrespecting your sisters, I find you wholly more interesting than a bunch of minor prophecies. Well, most of them... I also don't have many left at the moment.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You have done very well. Or rather you have done what would inevitably come to pass... although I choose to think that this result required much intention on your part.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What happens if I have no further prophecies I can fulfill?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps then there shall be another prophecy for me... to pass the Fated List along to someone else.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: No minor prophecies remain for me that have not come to pass. I've not seen new ones show up in a while, and... sounds like I shouldn't be expecting more anytime soon...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: If that is true, then my own purpose for arriving at these Crossroads is achieved. But now I do not know how to proceed, with neither my sisters nor the List to direct me.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Your sisters did suggest you may have had another purpose here, beyond ensuring that the Fated List is safe and everything within it gets checked off.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: And what might that have been, to violate their will repeatedly while growing close to you? My sisters like to scheme, but not to that extent.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: You're not in any rush to leave, are you?

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: On the contrary, I would be with you here as long as I can.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The rain is quite refreshing here, and strange. It never rains where I am from. A contribution from your grandmother to keep the Crossroads verdant, I suppose.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I learned I was related to Lady Demeter when I was young, and always thought of her whenever it did rain. She and my mother seem so different. I wonder how they got on.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Knowing how it is between the generations in our families, it's possible they didn't get along that well. But your mother would regularly visit at least, I understand.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: I'll try to do the same.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Hello Princess, I, um. I trust that when we spoke quite recently that I did not make you, uh, well...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What is it, Lord Moros? I've enjoyed our conversations lately, so you needn't fear. I'd tell you if I thought something was wrong. I'd ask you do the same.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: And I shall... except, in this case, I believe I misconstrued something... I thought you may have thought, because, admittedly I am a little new at all of this.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: My own experience in such matters is significantly lacking, too. As long as we stumble together, I suppose.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: I, too, suppose. Um, you be well tonight.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I have been thinking more about what you said at the taverna, Princess. About finding one's source of motivation, whether through encouraging results or through adversity. Or more Ambrosia.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh I wasn't being serious about the Ambrosia part. It's just, I find it remarkable sometimes that adversity and negativity spur me more than expressions of faith and support.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Hah! As a force of adversity and negativity myself, I appreciate your open-mindedness. But I wanted to say that Ambrosia's quite a motivator, too. I trust we can share some again sometime.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I have a bit of an awkward question, Princess... what is the custom here for making declarations of affection? I did try asking Dora first, but she didn't give me practical advice.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Erm... declarations of affection... I'm afraid I have bad news in that there is no custom for that here. You just choose the moment to express yourself, and hope your feelings are reciprocated.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I see. Well then, I'll have to choose a moment very carefully. I appreciate the information and your patience.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros... erm, so how are you this eve?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, I... was going to ask you the same. As for me, it's... well, it's as though I'm in a dream. The wonderful kind I've sometimes heard described. But, what of you...?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm very well, yes. My dreams... have often haunted me. Though I've experienced the good kind, too. And you're right, it's kind of like this... except the feeling never lasts.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps that shall be the difference in what we have; that it can last. If you would like, of course.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: We'll just have to find out.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros... erm, so how are you this eve?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess! I... was going to ask you the same thing. As for me... I'm doing well, pending your response to the same question. In any case, I'm grateful that we had a chance to speak the way we did.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm grateful, too. So... we remain close acquaintances then, right?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Of course. Although, I haven't had a lot of close acquaintances given my work, so... forgive me if I stumble.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You said before your sister Clotho revealed to you that we would meet, though you already knew you were promised to another. I suppose I've complicated things for you...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: And I am grateful that you did. I had no expectations for our meeting, but the truth is, I was drawn to you quite early on. Certainly in fewer than, say... five Nectar bottles.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Just because you're drawn to me, doesn't mean you won't also be drawn to Eos if and when you finally meet. From all I've heard, the break of Dawn is very beautiful.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, let us never speak of Dawn again. Perhaps my sisters know what shall become of me, but I increasingly suspect that they do not. They certainly did not prepare me for your beauty, for one.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, I ought to mention, I know there are others whom you're close with, all of whom I think you've known longer than me. I, on the other hand, have no one else. Living with my sisters, I had little opportunity.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I can think of no other explanation. Many different goddesses or gods would surely be delighted if they had you in their lives, Moros. But if I'm to have you to myself for now, that's certainly all right.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: I'm pleased you do not mind.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How many of your other siblings do you know? Besides the ones I've met. I've heard of so many others... Momus, the Keres, Oizys... she sounds like a lot.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I've met most of them, I think, at one time or another. We very seldomly have family get-togethers, something Mother Nyx decided to try after Olympus and your father tried to make a similar tradition out of it. But that did not go well.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How come? Wait, I know the answer to that. So, tell me this: Who was the worst behaved other than Strife?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well... you named several top contenders already.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Are you all right, Princess? I'm realizing that whenever you return, it's likely due to some unfortunate result. One I might have easily anticipated in the past, but not anymore...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I'm fine, Lord Moros, thank you for asking. I'm fairly accustomed to such outcomes at this point. It's interesting to know the Fates are not forcing it along... that it's all in my control. And all my fault.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well... now that you mention it, I can't entirely be sure the Fates have no more influence at all. How frustrating, not knowing whether our actions have been preordained or not...

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: I try not to think on it too much.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You must know what I'm about to ask, Lord Moros... especially since I promised many questions. You mentioned you were on a fruitless search. What was it that you sought?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Oh! Well, the Fates do like to send me off to fetch them this or that, now and again. My search in some respects was similar. Though, I'm beginning to think they tricked me...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: They sent you to find something that couldn't be found... and you can't just tell me what it is, can you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I think they simply wanted me out of the way. Although, I shouldn't speculate, or further speak of this at all... apologies.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: When you and I first met, you were searching for the Fates. But you discovered me among the shadows. I can navigate back to the Crossroads that way... but you can go farther?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I can, yes. I'm from the deepest reaches of the Underworld, where there exists no light at all. The chasm from which all creation sprang a while back.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I suppose even if one knew the Fates resided in such a place, they might still prove difficult to find...

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Difficult, though not impossible, as we now know...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The Oath of the Unseen is somewhere here, isn't that so, Princess...? The aura that it emanates... I haven't felt it since before all this. I thought the Oath was lost with Mother Nyx, but it was in the care of Lady Hecate.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It's in my own care now. The will of Night shall be done no matter what, isn't that so? You're here among the Unseen; you know our charge.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: The Oath is clearly in good hands.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It's such a fearsome night. On such occasions the Three Fates and I would do some of our finest work, I think.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You mean by sending mortals to some sort of unexpected but ultimately warranted demise. Did doing so involve the Oath of the Unseen?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Most likely, yes. I didn't speak the vows myself, but did experience some of the results. When mortals perish in some sort of dramatic fashion, it tends to make for reverent and obedient Shades.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Considering how well you seem to know Odysseus, surely you know brave Commander Schelemeus, do you not? Brave and humble, for I scarce know anything about his past.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Schelemeus, you say...? I know of no one by that name. And my recollection is infallible. Perhaps he hails from a distant land, presided over by its own strange gods...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Thank you for visiting me last night, Lord Moros. So, hypothetically, if I were to complete a certain incantation that somehow loosened the grip the Three Fates have on my birthright... how angry would they be?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Please understand, Princess, that I am not at liberty to give advice on matters such as that, not even hypothetically. I trust I've been sufficiently direct in putting it this way?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: In other words, we shouldn't be talking about this at all, and I assume all risk for my actions related to any potentially forbidden incantations that I've learned.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Princess, officially I cannot condone your actions. Your visits to the surface violate the terms the Fates set for your father's lineage, and you invite whichever consequences they see fit.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Yes, but unofficially, Lord Moros? If you'll permit yourself to speak of it this once. Or are we limited to candid conversations only when I'm on the verge of death?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Our conversations are most definitely more secure that way, but unofficially... I am amazed by your accomplishment. Perhaps living so close to the surface predisposed you, but still... I was uncertain it was possible.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: May the Fates forgive my transgressions.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I have to ask you something, Lord Moros, though I think I may already have the answer. Has the war with Chronos and Olympus taken some new turn?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm sorry I can't answer you, Princess. I'm bound never to speak of matters in connection to the weavings of the Fates. If you knew more right now, it could unduly influence your path.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: And...? Knowledge is preparation. It seems strictly to our benefit for me to know as much as possible about what I'm getting myself into.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps. Nevertheless, I cannot break my oath. I trust, though, that you'll learn the truth of it when the time is right.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: That Record Keeper over at the taverna must have served your father. And the Sage appears to have experience with prophecy. You've assembled quite a retinue.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I've always gotten on with Shades. Sometimes I think I understand them better than living beings. They can be easier for me to read.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I make most social inferences from a mix of speech, both tone and substance, and the speaker's small movements. With Shades, I can't discern these things at all.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Are you familiar with the Gifts of the Veil, Lord Moros? Bestowed on Night's Champion for dedicated service to the realm, having sworn a lot of Vows.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'm well aware of them indeed. My sisters wove the Gifts themselves; considered them some of their finest work. Why do you ask?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Merely curious. And when you say they wove the Gifts, you don't mean sculpting, metallurgy, anything like that?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: No, Princess. What you insinuate is causing me some slight concern. Did something happen to the Gifts?

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Oh not at all! I think.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: My sisters told me of a certain prophecy even I thought would never come to pass... that Olympus would once again face the fury of the Father of All Monsters, and their conflict would shake the world to its core. And now it's happening...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You mean you knew Typhon's attack was a real possibility? Why didn't you warn us? We could have prepared!

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I... was not permitted to warn you. Had I done so, it merely would have expedited an outcome I now realize was bound to happen anyway. Though perhaps your implication is correct, and this is all my fault...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: What? How is this all your fault? Unless you aided Chronos in awakening the most notorious fiend the gods ever faced, I don't see how you had anything to do with this.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Unraveling a Fateful Bond. I abetted your plans to gain the surface. The awakening of Typhon... perhaps it's a punishment... and a reminder never to defy the Fates. Princess, please forgive me.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Nonsense. Chronos did this, not you.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The prophecy concerning Typhon's reawakening... what made you think it would never come to pass? I thought the weavings of your sisters were always certain to come true.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The Fates are not always straightforward. Their prophecies are represented visually, not written in detail... at least the major ones. I thought my interpretation of Typhon's return was likely misguided.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: So the Fates wove the image of some terrible monstrosity climbing Olympus and the gods all desperately trying to fight back, and you thought it meant what?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well it wasn't that, exactly, though I didn't take it quite as literally as I should have in hindsight. I thought it prophesized that the Olympians would have to stand against some sort of existential threat...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: The prophecy of Typhon's return... was your interpretation that the gods were going to prevail or not? Or was there any detail you recall that we might use to our advantage?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well there was lightning, which I took to represent Zeus; the earth seemed to shake, recalling Poseidon; and a red spear reminiscent of your father's, piercing the storm. But even these specifics don't seem to pertain to the matter at hand.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: It's true, isn't it? You scoured Typhon from this world. The prophecy I saw must have... not merely been about his reawakening, but his eradication. How did you manage this, if I may ask?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: A complicated incantation from Headmistress, alongside the essence of Gigaros, my father's spear he took up after Chronos first was overthrown. It all worked out.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Your father's spear... in what I saw, a spear could be seen piercing through the storm. Then that must have been you. And so, the Fates truly did anticipate that you'd prevail.

endLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
endLines.text: Do they anticipate I shall slay Chronos next?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I wonder, did the weavings of the Fates foretell that I would knock the Father of All Monsters down the mountain last night? He didn't see it coming at least.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: No, not as such. The weavings of the Fates tend not to be precise in their detail, which I know can make them a bit confounding. But I salute your victory, Princess! I'd like to think that I foretold it, even if the Fates did not.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros, you must know something of the Nocturnal Arms. So, your mother and sisters collaborated on these weapons to be used only in dire need? And entrusted them to Headmistress?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Mother Nyx knew the fickle nature of the Olympians and the Fate-forged weapons that they used to seal away the Titans. She desired a set of counterparts that would awaken only if the realm itself was in upheaval.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I suppose there ought to be another set of weapons forged to keep these in check. And another for those, and so on.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Now you are being humorous, Princess.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Always wanted to ask this about the Fates, Lord Moros... why the weaving? Of all the ways one could foretell what's to come... I mean why not compose ballads, paint vases?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You'd think I'd know the answer, Princess, but it pains me to admit... I never once stopped to consider that. Weaving is just what they did. There aren't too many activities fit for exactly three. Perhaps that's it.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It just seems like they could make their prophecies much faster if they chose a simpler craft.

endLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
endLines.text: Best not to give them any ideas.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: There's so much here for me to learn! Lady Hecate has been generous enough to teach me something of the herbal ways and of your craft. An everlasting pursuit! I'm getting a late start.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You wish to become a witch, Lord Moros? Maybe I can give you some pointers sometime.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Have you experienced our hot springs yet, Lord Moros? The waters cleanse the body and the mind. I'm headed over if you'd care to join me?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Why, I... I don't see why not! I've seen and heard about such springs as these, though never been.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...To think that such a place is hidden here...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: So, what did you and the Fates do for recreation where you're from, anyhow?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Have I mentioned that they liked to weave? Their weaving was their occupation and their passion. I never got too good at it myself, and all they needed was the three of them, so...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I know an excellent spinstress who could maybe help with your technique. What did you do while they were busy spinning away?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Well, I would travel the world! To witness mortals in their final moments. Look them square in the face as they perished... often in horror...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...You do this for recreation's sake?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: The nature of the role leaves little time for recreation. And it's all I could do to make those final moments dignified... though I don't think the gesture always comes across.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I've not felt so refreshed in... well, it's been a while now. Thank you again.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Lord Moros, would you accompany me to the springs again sometime? And by sometime I mean... are you busy now?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I feared you'd never ask again, Princess! I've longed for the warmth of the springs since last we visited.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...I surely can get used to this...

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Your relationship with the Fates sounds like it must have been a complicated one. You've never been on your own, have you?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I haven't. They're my sisters, and my elders. I grew up under their watchful eye, and I'm more than their brother; I was their guardian. But I couldn't protect them, in the end. Perhaps they always knew I'd fail them, and loved me anyway.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You can't go thinking like that. You had one of the most important responsibilities in the world. The Fates are as all-knowing as they come, aren't they? Then it stands to reason there wasn't anybody better suited for the task than you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...Princess? Would you just let me wallow in my misery a bit? Ah... no, wait. Forget it. It's gone. A mighty incantation on your part just now.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Heh, anyway. I know you love your sisters, but... the circumstances being what they are, perhaps some distance will be good for you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps.... These springs are good for me, of that I have no doubt.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I think I really needed that. And always wonderful to have your company.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: How about a visit to the springs again, Moros? I'm not in any hurry at the moment if you're not either.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I'd like that very much. It's very peaceful there... almost like when we meet during the moment you return.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...I think it's possible the Fates arranged all this. Between the two of us. And I mean all of it. Has that occurred to you?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Perhaps a little, but it's something I refuse to believe. Why would the Fates have promised me to another if they wanted me to be with you? That makes very little sense.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I know, it's just... sometimes to get someone to do something, it's best to compel them to do some opposite thing they don't want to do. They'll do what you truly want in defiance. A simple bit of psychic manipulation.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: But I always was obedient. They had no reason to think I'd defy them... no, I just can't accept we were tricked into this somehow. We may not have met by chance, but still. My sisters had more important things to do.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I suppose it doesn't really matter. But if they did determine you and I should meet, I'm grateful. They could have chosen someone far less suitable.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: They certainly have their way. And I have never felt more grateful to them, or to you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: ...Whew! I think I'm ready for whatever else the Fates may have in store. See you, Melinoë.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: I was thinking about a visit to the hot springs and thought you might be interested, my lord.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Respectfully, Princess, I've past events to document so I had better not. Some other time, perhaps?

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: I'd more than welcome visiting the springs again with you.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: There's nothing I'd like better than to join you at the springs again.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: Your wish in this case is the same as mine. To the springs!

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: I feared that you would never ask again.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: I would be more than happy to oblige.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: That would be wonderful right now. Shall we?

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: Now that you mention it, I'd like that very much.

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...This is nice...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Could stay here all night...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Let our worries melt away...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...I needed this...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Whew, it's been a time...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Warmer than usual tonight...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...So peaceful here...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Just what I needed...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Whew, this is good...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...Always pleasant...

options.speaker: PlayerUnit
options.text: ...<Sigh> It's nice here...

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...It's an even greater comfort being here than in the shadows beyond space and time.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...This is a good place to discard whichever worries we are carrying around.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...The warmth and comfort of these waters never ceases to amaze me.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...May all our concerns wash away for now.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...The temperature is always perfect here.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...I really could stay here all night, I think.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...To cleanse the body is to cleanse the mind.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: That was refreshing, and a genuine delight.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: ...I feel entirely restored, and it was a pleasure as always.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: Whew! I could have stayed there all night long I think.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: A pleasure every single time, Princess.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: I must have really needed that. Thank you.

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: Well, that was quite refreshing, wasn't it?

options.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
options.text: I quite enjoyed that, and always grateful for your company.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I apologize for the abruptness of my approach. My mind... told me not to appear before you without good cause, but my heart demanded otherwise. Is this... not a good time?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh, you know, Lord Moros, I... was just in the middle of evading my latest brush with death. Why are you here? <Scoff> I must look like hell...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: No, you look... well, I am truly at a loss for words... I should have rehearsed what I sought to say more thoroughly.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: You don't need to be so nervous around me, or so formal for that matter. And it's nice to see you here as well, in one of my many moments of wretched failure. How long can we stay here, anyway? It's so peaceful...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: We can stay for... I don't exactly know. A while, that to others shall seem merely an instant, so... I sought to see you here again, in the off chance... you sought to see me...?

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Choice_MorosBecomingCloser01

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...Chance has nothing to do with it, Lord Moros. Most nights I have a brush with death, but not with you. I have wanted this, yes.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: ...Lord Moros, are... are you certain this shall only seem an instant by the time we return? Because...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: You have my word. As for your own sense of time, that I cannot change. So, as much as I would like remaining here with you eternally... we should probably be getting back.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Probably... but my heart says we can stay a little longer. And my mind has no coherent arguments to the contrary.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: On that, you'll hear no arguments from me, either.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: It is the height of flattery that you would feel this way, Lord Moros. But... in listening to my own heart once again... it's not my wish to risk or change what we already have. I know you must be disappointed, but I hope you understand.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: No, not disappointed in the least, Princess. And I do understand. Forgive me, once again, this outburst of feeling... I don't wish to pretend I never asked, but... may we perhaps still carry on as close acquaintances?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: If it were any other way, I'd never forgive myself, nor you.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: Hah! Well then, may you recover as swiftly as ever, and I shall see you shortly on the other side. Farewell for now.

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: I apologize for the abruptness of my approach. My mind... told me not to appear before you without good cause, but my heart demanded otherwise. Is this... not a good time?

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit
dialogueLines.text: Oh, you know, Lord Moros... I was just about to turn in after another night of furious conflict. So what brings you here? <Scoff> I must look like hell...

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
dialogueLines.text: On that, you'll hear no arguments from me, either.

dialogueLines.speaker: PlayerUnit

dialogueLines.speaker: NPC_Moros_01
